text:
This study examined the development and impact of theological education in The Apostolic Church Nigeria (TACN) from 1931 till date. The study adopted historical and qualitative research methods to examine the progression of the church from the informal apprenticeship system of Bible study and evangelism to the formalization of Theological Seminaries in Nigeria and Republic of Benin. The study examined the extent to which institutions such as LAWNA Theological Seminary contributed to uniformity of doctrine, effective ministry and academic development of church leaders.

While training has brought significant improvement in terms of pastoral quality and the ability of the Church to engage with contemporary ministry challenges, several challenges remain. The Church continues to experience financial challenges, infrastructure and technology issues as well as a cultural tension between academic study and the more spontaneous and charismatic forms of Pentecostal experience. In order for the training program of the TACN to be equipped to meet the future challenges it will have to invest heavily in the development of its lecturers as well as in the infrastructure required for the on-line provision of resources. The study provides a rich case study of one of Africa’s largest Pentecostal denominations and will be of great use to other Pentecostal denominations wishing to develop professional forms of leadership training that remain true to their formational theology.
